convergences: All five lenses independently land on the identical form factor: an open-source, local-first, single static binary that is simultaneously a CLI and an MCP server, providing O(1) copy-on-write fork / checkpoint / diff / promote / discard over the user's REAL working directory — including untracked files and bash side-effects — with no FUSE, no daemon, no signup, no server.

All five reject the same three head-on plays for the same reasons: hosted POSIX filesystem (Archil's + now AWS's capital game), hosted versioned FS (Mesa's game, unwinnable on trust for a solo vendor), and headless git API for platforms (Pierre's $23M moat, wrong sales motion). Four of five also independently reject the session-manager UI layer as commoditized and harness-absorbed.

The wedge gap is identically diagnosed everywhere: harness-native checkpoints (Claude Code /rewind) cover only the model's edits and die with the session; git worktrees cover only git-tracked files. Durable cross-session timelines, whole-directory forks, and undo of agent bash damage are unserved — and the individual harness power user is the one customer none of the three funded incumbents addresses.

Customer #1 converges across PM and customer lenses: the engineer running 3–8 parallel Claude Code/Codex sessions and fork-N-compare-promote loops, currently duct-taping cp -r, shell scripts, and worktrees. Crucially, the agent itself is a first-class user — verb-named MCP tools, --json on every command, and error messages that state the corrective next action.

Distribution consensus: OSS + the MCP/hooks channel ('claude mcp add agentspaces', .mcp.json, the API-frozen MCP registry) is the only channel a 1-person team owns, and none of Archil/Mesa/Pierre occupies it. Open source is not ideology here — it is simultaneously the distribution strategy, the trust strategy (no one bets a state layer on a closed solo vendor), and the only durable defense against incumbent bundling.

Git interop at the edge, never a git wire-protocol server: every checkpoint exportable as a commit, promote lands as an ordinary git branch/PR, and state is never trapped in a proprietary format. Architect, scout, and skeptic all explicitly kill the idea of speaking git's wire protocol as a server.

Zero custody in v1 and a content-addressed, sync-ready on-disk format from day one: BYO disk now, BYO S3/R2 bucket sync as the first expansion (conditional-write ref CAS means no service to operate), hosted open-core control plane only after trust and adoption exist. Approach the report's Section-5 primitive from below, not from a datacenter.

Identical non-negotiables on quality and risk: data loss is a one-strike permanent kill for a storage-adjacent tool, so a published crash-safety torture suite (kill -9 matrix, fault injection) ships with v1; and the performance bar is unambiguous — fork + status must be sub-second on a multi-GB, 100k-file dirty tree or the pitch dies in the first demo.

Turso AgentFS is named by four lenses as the nearest collision, with the same differentiation each time: AgentFS is a local SQLite-file substrate, not git-interoperable and not durable to cloud; agentspaces is the workflow/version layer over real directories and real repos, git-native and sync-ready. The seam AgentFS has published intentions toward but not shipped is exactly the target — which makes speed a strategy, not a virtue.

The killer demo is the same in every lens: fork the workspace N ways, run N agent sessions, get a side-by-side diff table, promote the winner, discard the rest — the human-approval gate expressed as a storage operation, which is the report's 'promotion as a recorded act' made tangible on a laptop.
conflicts: Engine internals — custom CAS vs git-native storage. The architect rejects the git object model internally (no chunking, packfile complexity) and specifies SQLite-as-truth + BLAKE3/FastCDC CAS; the prior-art scout and skeptic argue for plain git plumbing / shadow-git capture first (gitoxide later), because everything stays recoverable with stock git. Adjudication: the scout and skeptic are right for v1. For a solo storage vendor, 'worst-case failure degrades to a plain git repo' is the single most valuable trust asset available (the skeptic's one-strike point), and git-native storage makes the moat-bearing interop free by construction. The architect's real concerns (large blobs, micro-commit churn) are handled by a FastCDC/BLAKE3 sidecar for big files, write-batching, and periodic repack — and by hiding the engine behind a Workspace trait so hot paths can move to gitoxide or a custom CAS when profiling, not aesthetics, demands it.

Exec in v1 — the architect wants a local exec driver plus two sandbox adapters by week 12; PM and customer lenses defer attached compute entirely ('sandboxes are partners, not product'). Adjudication: split the difference at the thin end. A minimal run-wrapper that records command, exit code, and duration into the journal and auto-checkpoints is in v1 — it is what makes 'commits are the audit log' literal and powers the race demo. Sandbox adapters (E2B/Modal/Daytona) and any exec service are out until post-v1; the report itself says compute is a verb, and verbs can be added later.

Sync timing — the architect ships BYO-bucket sync inside the 6-week window; skeptic and customer lenses push it to a v2 fast-follow after local trust is earned; the PM puts hosted at month 4–6. Adjudication: the skeptic wins on sequencing, the architect wins on design. The format is content-addressed and sync-ready from day one (this is load-bearing and cheap), but zero-server BYO-bucket sync is the first post-v1 milestone at weeks 5–8, not a v1 component — shipping a flawless local engine in 4 weeks beats shipping a distracted one with sync in 6. Hosted multi-tenant anything is gated on the custody and runway forks below.

Timeline realism — the architect claims production-grade local-first at 6 weeks; the scout says 8–12 weeks; the skeptic demands a credible v1 with published torture tests inside ~10 weeks. Adjudication: split 'demoable' from 'trusted.' A dogfoodable alpha inside Claude Code by week 2 and a soft-launched v1 at week 4 are realistic on the git-plumbing engine; the word 'production-grade' is not used publicly until the torture suite, benchmark suite, and 4+ weeks of dogfooding exist (weeks 8–10). The 4-week sketch below reflects alpha-to-soft-launch, not GA.

Persona ordering — the customer lens ranks the eval/fleet engineer above the Claude Code daily-driver; the PM merges them; the customer lens itself then warns that a solo founder can only feel one workflow. Adjudication: they are the same artifact and nearly the same person; polish the dogfooded Claude Code daily-driver loop first (the founder lives in it, so taste is felt rather than guessed) and co-design the diff/promote comparison UX with 2–3 recruited eval-heavy users — the customer lens's own hybrid option.

Mount ambition — the architect keeps a month-4 FUSE/NFS go/no-go alive (with a 1-week read-only NFSv3 loopback spike); customer and skeptic lenses say cede mounts and isolation permanently to sandboxes and Archil-class vendors. Adjudication: not a v1 question and not yet a founder fork — default to staying the version-semantics/workflow layer, spend the 1-week NFSv3 spike at month 4 only if traction exists, and treat 'become a filesystem company' as a decision to re-open with data, not now. It is deliberately excluded from the founder forks below because the cheap probe makes it reversible.

decision_forks.question: What is agentspaces for, ultimately: a venture-scale run at the agent state layer, a profitable independent dev-tool business, or a serious open-source project whose business shape is decided later?

options.label: Venture path
options.implication: Raise a pre-seed/seed on the wedge thesis within ~3 months of launch; race to the hosted open-core tier and the full Section-5 primitive; accept the custody business, hiring, and the obligation to compete with Mesa/Archil for the layer rather than coexist.

options.label: Independent business
options.implication: Bootstrap to revenue via prosumer/team sync subscriptions; stay small; optimize for a beloved tool with durable maintenance over land-grab speed; acquisition interest from Mesa/Archil becomes a live exit rather than a distraction.

options.label: Earn-the-decision optionality
options.implication: Commit only to making the OSS wedge undeniable; instrument the 90-day metrics (installs, weekly actives, forks/week, retention, hosted waitlist) and decide raise-vs-bootstrap at month 3 with evidence instead of forecasts. Costs some speed if venture is the eventual answer; preserves both doors.

decision_forks.rationale: Every lens agrees the first 90 days look identical regardless of endgame: same artifact, same OSS launch, same metrics. The PM and customer lenses both independently propose deferring this call to real adoption data, and the skeptic's red-team shows the venture path's custody obligations are exactly the early risks to avoid. The only thing that must be decided NOW to preserve optionality is the license (next fork) — so decide that, ship, and let 90-day evidence price the ambition. If Arnav already knows he wants venture scale, choose it openly: it pulls fundraising prep and the hosted design forward and changes nothing about v1.

decision_forks.question: How many months of zero revenue will you fund, given this plan is realistically $0 until a hosted/sync tier exists (month 4–6 earliest, month 6–9 to first real payers)?

options.label: 12+ months funded
options.implication: Run the plan as written: pure OSS through 2026, BYO-bucket sync as a free feature, monetize from strength in 2027. The strongest version of the strategy and the one all five lenses implicitly assume.

options.label: ~6 months funded
options.implication: Pull a thin paid tier (hosted sync control plane: Worker + R2, or paid prosumer sync at $10–20/mo) forward to month 3–4, accepting a rougher OSS v1 and roughly halved engine velocity during the squeeze.

options.label: Under 6 months
options.implication: Per the skeptic, bottom-up OSS infra has a 12–24 month gestation — on this horizon the honest move is to either raise money now on the wedge thesis before writing much code, or pick a different, demo-shaped project. Do not start this plan hoping the clock is wrong.

decision_forks.rationale: The skeptic's analysis is the decisive one: the competitive clocks (Turso's disaggregation trajectory, harness-native checkpointing deepening every release) reward shipping fast, but the adoption clock for trust-sensitive storage tooling cannot be compressed below ~12 months. Building the monetization layer early loses both races — the PM, customer, and skeptic lenses all converge on this exact failure mode. Only Arnav knows his bank account; but the plan should only be started on a horizon that lets it work.

decision_forks.question: What license ships on the engine — a decision that is effectively irreversible after launch?

options.label: MIT/Apache core, declared open-core line
options.implication: Maximum adoption and trust (the only posture that makes a solo vendor adoptable as a state layer); the format can become the standard harnesses and session managers build on. You accept that Turso, a hyperscaler, or Mesa could ship your work as a service — the defense is pace, format ownership, and owning the sync/control plane you declare proprietary from day one.

options.label: BUSL/FSL source-available
options.implication: Legally protects the future hosted tier from competitive hosting; costs real adoption friction with exactly the OSS-native personas who are the entire distribution plan, and blunts the 'become the standard' defense against bundlers.

options.label: Closed binary, generous free tier
options.implication: Fastest theoretical path to charging; per the customer lens, likely fatal — neither eval engineers nor Claude Code power users will adopt a closed state layer from a one-person company, and the channel dies.

decision_forks.rationale: Four of five lenses independently conclude permissive is the strongest fit: it is the distribution channel (PM), the only defensible posture against bundling (architect), the trust prerequisite for the paying persona (customer), and the bus-factor mitigation (skeptic). The free-rider risk is real but second-order: the engine is weeks of work for anyone, so the moat was never the code — it is format adoption, integration depth, and pace. Declare the open-core boundary (engine and format open forever; hosted sync/control plane proprietary) in the README at launch so the community never feels rug-pulled.

decision_forks.question: How hard do you ride Claude Code — the best distribution channel available and also the vendor most likely to absorb the feature?

options.label: Claude-Code-first, hedged
options.implication: Deep hooks integration (PreToolUse/PostToolUse auto-checkpoints around Bash/Edit) and polished MCP experience ship in v1; the CLI/MCP contract stays strictly harness-neutral underneath; a Codex/OpenCode port is a named, dated milestone (~week 6, where an open Codex feature request for exactly this already exists). Fastest time-to-wow with a priced, bounded absorption risk.

options.label: Ride Claude Code maximally
options.implication: All polish into the Anthropic surface, ports whenever. Deepest single-channel love and fastest initial growth; a single Anthropic release note covering bash side-effects and untracked files compresses the wedge to near zero.

options.label: Strictly harness-agnostic
options.implication: MCP + CLI only, no harness-specific hooks anywhere. Most durable against absorption; dilutes the v1 experience, slows time-to-wow, and forfeits the one distribution advantage a solo founder has.

decision_forks.rationale: PM and skeptic both land on the hedge independently. The durable layers — cross-session durability, whole-directory forks, git interop, and eventually sync — are precisely the things a harness vendor won't build (they are cross-harness and cross-machine by nature), so deep integration is rentable distribution while the defensible value accretes underneath. The hedge converts 'kill zone' from an existential bet into a scheduled diversification.

decision_forks.question: Will you ever operate a hosted storage service — on-call rotation of one, personal liability for other people's bytes — and if so, on what condition?

options.label: Custody after revenue funds ops
options.implication: v1 and the sync tier stay zero-custody (BYO S3/R2 bucket, user's keys, thin stateless control plane at most); a true hosted tier ('GitHub for agent workspaces') waits until revenue or funding pays for a second engineer and real ops. Caps near-term revenue shape to prosumer/control-plane pricing; keeps the catastrophic-failure surface at zero while trust is being earned.

options.label: Zero custody forever
options.implication: The business ceiling becomes support, sponsorship, and control-plane subscriptions; the venture endgame effectively closes; in exchange, the one risk that kills storage companies overnight is permanently off the table.

options.label: Custody as the plan
options.implication: Commit now to the hosted tier as the product, design billing/multi-tenancy early, and accept that a solo operator selling custody of strangers' code pre-track-record is the single position every lens rated unwinnable.

decision_forks.rationale: The skeptic frames this exactly right: every serious monetization path eventually runs through custody, so pretending the question doesn't exist is how architectures get built wrong. The BYO-bucket design (conditional-write ref CAS on the user's own S3/R2) is the elegant middle — it delivers multi-machine durability and even team sharing with zero bytes custodied, keeps the hosted door open because the format and sync protocol are already proven, and defers the on-call-of-one liability until there is a team. Answering this now also settles what the architect needs: tokens designed into the API, unenforced until a control plane exists.

synthesis.proposed_v1: Agentspaces v1 is a single static binary, `asp`, that is simultaneously a CLI and an MCP stdio server, MIT/Apache-licensed, macOS (APFS) and Linux (btrfs/XFS, degraded fallback elsewhere) — no FUSE, no daemon, no account, no telemetry. `asp init` adopts any existing directory or git repo without disturbing it (sidecar store, never rewrites the user's history); `asp fork [-n N]` produces O(1) clonefile/reflink copies of the WHOLE working directory — untracked files, .env, build artifacts included; a shadow-git capture layer (plain git plumbing behind a Workspace trait, FastCDC/BLAKE3 sidecar for large blobs) records every change as a commit-grained checkpoint with an append-only journal mapping each checkpoint to the tool call, session, and prompt that caused it, all recoverable with stock git plumbing by design. The core loop: fork per agent attempt → auto-checkpoint around every change (Claude Code PreToolUse/PostToolUse hooks make bash side-effects rewindable — the documented gap in /rewind) → durable cross-session timeline and undo → `asp diff` cross-fork comparison table → `asp promote` lands the winner as an ordinary git branch/PR, `asp discard` deletes the rest; `asp race "<prompt>" -n 3` packages the whole loop as the demo. The MCP server exposes workspace_fork / checkpoint / diff / promote / undo / log with agent-legible descriptions, --json on every command, and error strings that state the corrective next action, so the agent itself is a first-class user across Claude Code, Codex, and OpenCode. Shipped alongside the binary: a kill -9 crash-safety torture suite in CI, honest published benchmarks (fork latency and disk overhead on a 5GB dirty 100k-file monorepo vs cp -r and git worktree), a 90-second README demo, and a 'why not git worktrees / why not AgentFS' doc. The on-disk format is content-addressed and sync-ready from day one, with capability-token fields present in the API but unenforced. Explicitly OUT of v1: any hosted service or custody of bytes, BYO-bucket sync (first post-v1 milestone, weeks 5–8), FUSE/NFS/mounts, sandbox exec adapters, token enforcement, Windows, web UI, the versioned-memory feature, session-manager UI, and all non-coding verticals.
synthesis.north_star: Agentspaces is the open-source, local-first state engine that turns every agent session into an instant, disposable, fully-reviewable fork of your real working directory — fork is control flow, the checkpoint journal is the audit log, and promote is the only way work lands.
first_four_weeks: Week 1 — Kill the two existential risks before building anything else: benchmark clonefile/reflink whole-directory fork plus mtime-indexed status on a real 5GB/100k-file dirty monorepo (target: sub-second, published numbers), and spike the shadow-git capture layer (untracked files, write-batching, large-blob sidecar) proving everything is recoverable with stock git. Scaffold `asp init/fork/checkpoint/diff/discard` behind the Workspace trait and write the one-page format doc that locks in content-addressed, sync-ready objects.

Week 2 — Demoable alpha inside Claude Code, dogfooded on agentspaces itself: hooks integration auto-checkpointing around every Bash/Edit tool call, MCP server with workspace_* verbs, --json on every command, corrective-action error strings. From this week forward, agentspaces is built using agentspaces — every rough edge is felt, not guessed.

Week 3 — Complete the loop and earn the trust claims: promote-to-git (winner lands as an ordinary branch/PR), durable cross-session timeline and undo, `asp race -n N` end-to-end, kill -9 crash-safety torture suite running in CI. Recruit 5–10 Claude Code power users and 2–3 eval/fleet engineers as weekly design partners; co-design the diff comparison table with the latter.

Week 4 — Polish the first five minutes and soft-launch: brew/npx/curl install, 90-second README demo (8 forks of a 5GB workspace in under a second, diff table, promote), published benchmarks vs cp -r and git worktrees, the 'why not worktrees / why not AgentFS' positioning docs, declared open-core license boundary. Ship to the recruited cohort, fix what they hit, and prep the public HN/X launch for week 5–6 — with the Codex/OpenCode port and BYO-bucket sync already scheduled as the next two milestones.

pm.summary: The three incumbents have carved up the hosted, platform-facing territory — Archil owns "POSIX mount, maximum speed," Mesa owns "every write versioned," Pierre owns "headless git for platforms" — but all three are closed, hosted, and sell to platforms or cloud workloads; none of them serves the individual harness power user working on their own machine, and none is open source. Meanwhile the harness ecosystem proves the demand locally: Claude Code ships native per-prompt checkpoints and worktree support, and an entire cohort of session managers (Conductor, Nimbalyst/ex-Crystal, parallel-code) exists purely to run parallel agents in worktrees — yet harness checkpoints explicitly do NOT cover bash commands or user edits and die with the session, and worktrees only version git-tracked files, forcing every user to hand-copy .env, node_modules, build caches, and data files, with no protection against an agent's `rm -rf` or migration script. The unoccupied ground is therefore the open-source, local-first workspace STATE ENGINE underneath harnesses and session managers: copy-on-write fork/checkpoint/diff/promote semantics over the WHOLE working directory (untracked files and bash side-effects included), git-interoperable, distributed as a CLI + Claude Code hooks + MCP server. Customer #1 is the staff-level engineer or solo technical founder running 3–8 Claude Code/Codex sessions daily on one repo, who today maintains shell scripts to clone untracked state into worktrees and has lost work to agent bash commands that /rewind cannot undo. The wedge is credibly "the primitive" and not a feature because it owns the state format and audit log that session managers, eval runners, and eventually the hosted tier all build on — the expansion path is local engine → integrations (session managers, CI, Codex/OpenCode) → open-core hosted sync ("GitHub for agent workspaces": durable named workspaces, lazy pull, team review) → the report's full Section-5 primitive, approached from below rather than from the datacenter. Open-core is the only model that fits a 1-person team: OSS is the sole realistic distribution channel against three funded closed products, and hosted sync at pay-nothing-at-rest pricing (free local forever; ~$0.15–0.20/GB-month on ACTIVE data only, zero egress on agent-path reads, generous free cold tier) is the monetization that the report shows every winner converges on. Success in 90 days looks like: 500+ real installs with ≥1 fork, 50 weekly-active users averaging ≥20 forks/week, week-4 retention ≥30%, p50 whole-workspace fork <500ms on a 5GB dirty repo, 2+ session-manager or harness-adjacent integrations, and a 100+ signup waitlist for hosted sync. What NOT to build, explicitly: no NVMe cache fleet or network filesystem (Archil's capital game), no hosted versioned-FS service as v1 (Mesa's game, pre-trust), no distributed git API for platforms (Pierre's moat, requires enterprise SLA a solo founder can't sign), no sandbox/compute substrate, and no session-manager UI (crowded, thin — power those tools instead of competing with them).

form_factor_options.option: Local-first OSS workspace engine: CLI + Claude Code hooks + MCP server (copy-on-write fork/checkpoint/diff/promote of the whole working directory)
pros: Genuinely unoccupied: all three incumbents are hosted/closed and ignore the individual harness user; harness-native checkpoints cover only model edits (not bash/user changes) and worktrees cover only git-tracked files — the gap is documented and felt daily
Distribution is free and immediate: ships as `npx`/brew install, a Claude Code hooks config, and an MCP server — lands directly inside the highest-engagement developer surface of 2026
Buildable to production quality by 1 person + AI: APFS clonefile / Linux reflink + a content-addressed journal is weeks of work, not Archil's protocol or Pierre's distributed refs
Open source counter-positions all three closed incumbents at once and makes the format adoptable by session managers (Conductor, Nimbalyst, parallel-code) as infrastructure rather than competition
Local-first means zero trust barrier — no one has to put their repo on a solo founder's servers
Directly embodies the report's primitive at small scale: forks are control flow, checkpoints+journal are the audit log, promote is the HITL gate
cons: Anthropic could deepen native checkpointing to cover bash and untracked files, compressing the wedge (they iterate fast on exactly this surface)
CoW portability tax: APFS and btrfs/XFS reflink are easy, ext4 and Windows are not — platform matrix eats solo-dev time
Zero revenue for months; monetization arrives only with the hosted tier
Thin technical moat at v1 — defensibility must come from format adoption, integration depth, and pace
form_factor_options.verdict: PURSUE — this is the v1; it is the only form factor where a 1-person team has both a real gap and a free distribution channel.

form_factor_options.option: Open-core hosted sync: durable named workspaces in the cloud, lazy pull to any machine/sandbox, team review UI ("GitHub for agent workspaces")
pros: The natural monetization of the local engine — push/pull workspaces like git remotes, billed pay-nothing-at-rest
Expansion path to the full Section-5 primitive (lazy materialization, data-scoped tokens, mount into sandboxes) approached from an installed base rather than cold
By the time it ships, the local format and audit log are already in users' muscle memory — the hosted tier sells itself to existing users
cons: Premature now: a solo founder hosting other people's code needs trust signals (SOC2, durability story) that take time
Building it first would mean competing with Mesa/Archil head-on with 1/20th the resources
Distracts from winning the local wedge, which is the whole strategy
form_factor_options.verdict: DEFER — design the local format so every object is content-addressed and sync-ready from day one; ship hosted around month 4–6 once weekly actives prove pull.

form_factor_options.option: Hosted versioned filesystem (mini-Mesa): FUSE/VFS mounts, every-write versioning as a cloud service
pros: Closest literal match to the report's primitive
Mesa is still in private beta, so a GA window technically exists
cons: Capital- and ops-intensive: durable multi-tenant storage, mount infrastructure, on-call — exactly what a 1-person team cannot run credibly
Head-on with a funded team (Mesa, $5M, ex-Microsoft/Census) and with Archil's GA product on the POSIX side
No trust story: nobody mounts production state from an unknown solo operator
Sales motion (platform/enterprise) mismatched to founder capacity
form_factor_options.verdict: REJECT — this is Mesa's game; entering it head-on with fewer resources and later timing is strictly dominated.

form_factor_options.option: Headless git API for platforms (mini-Pierre)
pros: Proven revenue pool (codegen platforms), clear buyer
Git interop is real moat-bearing territory per the report
cons: Pierre has ~$23M, years of distributed-git systems work, ex-GitHub help, and existing platform deals — the moat is exactly the part a newcomer can't speed-run
Platform customers demand SLAs, scale proof, and vendor diligence a solo founder fails by default
Concedes the 'agents just want files' interface thesis the rest of the strategy is built on
form_factor_options.verdict: REJECT — strongest incumbent moat of the three; nothing about a 1-person team improves the attack.

form_factor_options.option: Session-manager / parallel-agent UI app (compete with Conductor, Nimbalyst, parallel-code)
pros: Visible, demo-friendly, directly monetizable as a prosumer app
Existing tools prove willingness to adopt
cons: Crowded and churning (Crystal already deprecated into Nimbalyst); differentiation is UI taste, not infrastructure
Harness vendors absorb this layer first (Claude Code desktop already auto-creates worktrees per session)
Positions agentspaces as a competitor to the very tools that should adopt the engine
form_factor_options.verdict: REJECT as a product; ship only a minimal fork-compare flow (`asp race`) as the demo of the engine, and court session managers as integration partners.

form_factor_options.option: Per-agent memory/workspace product (versioned memory for harness users)
pros: Memory is a rising, well-understood pain; versioned memory directories with rollback/audit is a real differentiator vs flat memory files
MCP-native distribution fits
cons: Memory as a standalone product is crowded and increasingly shipped natively by harnesses
Small-file, low-byte workloads make the storage engine overkill as the headline
It's a feature of the workspace (a versioned subtree that survives forks), not a wedge
form_factor_options.verdict: DEFER / fold in — ship `~/.agentspaces/memory` as a versioned, fork-surviving subtree feature once the engine exists; never lead with it.

must_have: Core CoW engine: `asp fork` / `asp checkpoint` / `asp diff` / `asp promote` / `asp discard` over the entire working directory including untracked files — APFS clonefile (macOS) first since harness power users skew Mac, btrfs/XFS reflink second, copy fallback elsewhere

Claude Code hooks integration: PreToolUse/PostToolUse auto-checkpoints around every Bash and Edit tool call, making bash side-effects rewindable — the exact documented gap in native checkpointing ('checkpoints apply to Claude's edits and not user edits or bash commands')
MCP server exposing fork/checkpoint/diff/promote/log so agents themselves (any MCP harness: Claude Code, Codex, OpenCode) can manage workspace state as tools

Git interop at the edge: a workspace wraps a repo; `promote` lands as a normal git branch/commit so CI, GitHub, and review work unchanged; engine also works in non-git directories (data folders, document workspaces)
Append-only audit journal: every change-producing tool call mapped to a checkpoint with timestamp, session, and prompt — 'commits are the audit log' made literal and greppable
One killer demo flow: `asp race "<prompt>" -n 3` — fork the workspace N ways, run N harness sessions, present side-by-side diffs, promote the winner, discard the rest
Content-addressed on-disk format designed for future sync (hash-stable objects, no machine-local assumptions) even though sync itself does not ship
Honest benchmarks published in the README: fork latency and disk overhead on a 5GB dirty node/python monorepo vs `git worktree add` + manual copying
defer: Hosted sync / remote workspaces and any server-side component (month 4–6, after weekly actives prove demand)
FUSE/NFS/network mounts and lazy materialization from remote
Attached compute (`exec`) of any kind — sandboxes are partners, not product
Data-scoped capability tokens, team RBAC, review UI
Windows support and ext4-without-reflink optimization
Per-syscall write interception (FSEvents/eBPF journaling) — per-tool-call granularity is enough for v1
Versioned agent-memory subtree as a named feature
Non-coding verticals (claims, contracts, data rooms) — the report says they're real, but they buy from vendors with SOC2, not from a repo
Merge of divergent forks beyond git-level merge on promote (jj-grade conflict semantics can come later)

risks: Channel-as-competitor (the big one): Anthropic iterates fast on exactly this surface — native checkpoints and worktree support already shipped (code.claude.com/docs/en/worktrees, platform.claude.com/docs/en/agent-sdk/file-checkpointing); extending checkpoints to bash side-effects and untracked files would compress the v1 wedge. Mitigation: be cross-harness (Codex has an open feature request for exactly this — openai/codex#12558), durable across sessions, git-interoperable, and own the sync/hosted layer harness vendors won't build.

Feature-not-product gravity: the v1 engine is weeks of work, so it is also weeks of work for anyone else; moat must be earned through format adoption, integration breadth, and the hosted tier — if adoption stalls, this collapses into a popular gist.

CoW portability fragmentation: macOS APFS is clean, but Linux ext4 lacks reflink, Docker volume drivers vary, and Windows is its own world — a solo dev can sink a quarter into the platform matrix; mitigate by being loudly macOS+btrfs/XFS-first.

Mesa squeeze from above: if Mesa GAs with a local/VFS mode and a free tier, it offers 'the same but with a cloud behind it'; the counters are open source, offline-first, and zero trust requirement — keep those genuinely true.

Session managers build instead of adopt: Conductor/Nimbalyst could each ship a bespoke snapshot layer; mitigate by making adoption trivially easier than building (stable lib API, permissive license for the client SDK).

Monetization lag: OSS-first means ~$0 revenue for 6+ months and hosted sync requires trust-building (SOC2-lite, durability story) before anyone pushes private code; founder runway is a hard constraint, not a footnote.

Solo-founder bus factor mirrored at Archil: the report flags single-founder risk for Archil — the same applies here for anyone evaluating agentspaces as a dependency; OSS partially mitigates (forkable), hosted tier reintroduces it.
Performance credibility: if fork on a real dirty 5GB monorepo isn't visibly instant (<1s), the whole pitch dies in the first demo; this is the one benchmark that must be unimpeachable before launch.

open_questions.question: Ambition and endgame: is agentspaces a venture-scale run at the state layer, or a profitable independent dev-tool business?
open_questions.why_it_matters: This decides the license (MIT/Apache maximizes adoption but lets Mesa/Archil/Anthropic absorb the format; BUSL/AGPL protects the hosted tier but slows the OSS flywheel), whether to raise money before the hosted tier, and how aggressively to chase the full Section-5 primitive versus staying a beloved tool.
options: Venture path: permissive core + raise a seed once weekly actives prove pull, race to hosted sync and the full primitive
Independent path: BUSL-style license, bootstrap to hosted revenue, stay small and profitable
Optionality path: permissive core now, defer the decision until 90-day metrics exist (costs some protection, preserves both doors)

open_questions.question: Bet concentration: Claude-Code-first with deep hooks integration, or harness-agnostic from day one?
open_questions.why_it_matters: Claude-Code-first gives the deepest integration, fastest user love, and the clearest demo — but maximizes exposure to Anthropic shipping the feature natively. Harness-agnostic (MCP-only, plus Codex/OpenCode) spreads the risk but dilutes the v1 experience and slows time-to-wow. Only the founder can price his own risk tolerance on a channel he doesn't control.
options: Claude-Code-first: hooks + MCP, port to Codex/OpenCode in month 2–3
Harness-agnostic from day one: MCP + CLI only, no harness-specific hooks
Claude-Code-first but with the Codex port as the explicit week-6 milestone (hedged concentration)

open_questions.question: Posture toward the incumbents: build toward partnership/acquisition, or declare independence and compete for the primitive?
open_questions.why_it_matters: Agentspaces-as-local-on-ramp is a natural acquisition or partnership for Mesa (local mode for their cloud FS) or Archil (local tier below the NVMe fleet); optimizing for that means format compatibility and warm relationships. Optimizing for independence means building the hosted tier yourself and treating them as competitors. The strategies diverge early — in license, in format design, and in whether you take their calls.
options: Independent: build the hosted tier, compete for the layer
Partner-curious: stay compatible (e.g., git-interop everywhere, jj-friendly internals), engage Mesa/Archil early, keep acquisition as a live exit
Ecosystem-neutral: also court Anthropic — position the format as the standard harnesses adopt, monetize sync only

open_questions.question: Runway: how many months of zero revenue is acceptable, and is there outside money on the table?
open_questions.why_it_matters: The plan has revenue ≈ $0 until the hosted tier (month 4–6 at the earliest, realistically month 6–9 to first paying teams). Whether that's fine, tight, or impossible determines whether to compress the OSS phase, consult part-time, or raise — and only the founder knows his bank account and appetite.
options: Self-funded 12+ months: run the plan as written
Self-funded 6 months: pull hosted sync forward to month 3, accept a rougher OSS v1
Raise a small angel/pre-seed round on the wedge thesis now, buy 18 months

pm.recommendation: Build agentspaces as the open-source, local-first branchable-workspace engine for agent harness users: a CLI + Claude Code hooks + MCP server that makes every agent session an instant copy-on-write fork of the WHOLE working directory (untracked files and bash side-effects included — the two documented gaps in native harness checkpoints and git worktrees), with checkpoint/diff/promote/discard semantics, an append-only audit journal, and git interop on promote. Customer #1 is the engineer running 3–8 parallel Claude Code/Codex sessions who today shell-scripts untracked state into worktrees and cannot undo an agent's bash damage. Win that user through OSS + MCP distribution (the channel none of Archil/Mesa/Pierre occupies and none can credibly enter as closed hosted vendors), make the on-disk format content-addressed and sync-ready from day one, and expand open-core into hosted workspace sync at pay-nothing-at-rest pricing (~$0.15–0.20/GB-month active data, zero agent-path egress, free local forever) around month 4–6 — approaching the report's Section-5 primitive from below, on the strength of an installed base, rather than from a datacenter you cannot afford. Explicitly do not build: a network filesystem, a hosted versioned FS at v1, a platform git API, any compute substrate, or a session-manager UI — power Conductor-class tools instead of competing with them.

architect.lens: Chief Architect — what a 1-person+AI team can actually ship to production, and in what order
architect.summary: The architecture question is not "which storage engine" but "which surface can one developer make production-grade while three funded teams (Archil ~$18M, Mesa ~$5M, Pierre ~$23M) build cloud services above us" — and the answer is the surface none of them occupy: local-first, open-installable, harness-native. All three competitors are cloud-service-first with waitlists and sales motions; nobody owns the "works inside Claude Code in 30 seconds, no account, no mount, no server" position, which is also the only position that survives the report's sharpest counter-thesis (incumbents/sandbox vendors bundling approximations of the primitive) because an open local engine becomes the format others adopt rather than the service they crush. Concretely: a single static Rust binary containing (1) a content-addressed engine — SQLite-as-truth for the commit DAG, tree manifests, refs, mtime index, and branch leases, plus a BLAKE3 CAS with FastCDC chunking for files >1MB and inline storage ≤4KB — where O(1) copy-on-write forking falls out for free because commits/trees/chunks are immutable and structurally shared, a branch is one row; (2) a built-in MCP stdio server exposing exactly the Section-5 verbs (open/fork/checkpoint/diff/merge/exec/grep) plus materialize-to-workdir so the harness's native file tools operate on a real directory while version semantics ride MCP; (3) exec-attached-to-state as fork→materialize→run-subprocess→record(cmd,exit,duration)→auto-checkpoint, with Docker and E2B/Modal/Daytona adapters later (inject the same static binary into the sandbox, checkout from bucket, push back) — we never build isolation; (4) git interop at the edges via gitoxide export/import, never a git wire-protocol server; (5) zero-server sync to a BYO S3/R2 bucket, with chunk upload as idempotent CAS replication and ref updates as compare-and-swap via S3 conditional writes ([If-Match PutObject, GA Nov 2024](https://aws.amazon.com/about-aws/whats-new/2024/11/amazon-s3-functionality-conditional-writes/)) — meaning multi-machine sync ships without operating a service. We deliberately reject the jj-lib backend (steal its semantics — fork-on-first-write, op log, eventually conflict-carrying changes — but own the bytes; jj-lib's API churn and human-VCS working-copy assumptions cost more than they buy, and Mesa owns that narrative) and reject the git object model internally (no chunking, tree-granularity, packfile complexity; git belongs at the edge). Lazy materialization is honest-but-approximate without a kernel layer: path-scoped checkout with chunk-level fetch and dedup, fully-lazy reads only via the SDK VFS — true fault-on-open laziness requires the FUSE/NFS bet, which is a deliberate month-4+ decision, not a v1 component. Capability tokens (workspace/branch-glob/path-glob/mode/ttl) are designed into the API from day one but unenforced while local-first; enforcement is meaningful only when a thin sync service exists (a Cloudflare Worker in front of R2 is the cheapest enforcement point), because plain-S3 sync cannot enforce path scoping. Timeline honesty: 2 weeks = demoable alpha inside Claude Code (not production); 6 weeks = production-grade local-first (kill -9 crash matrix in CI, sub-second status on 100k files, GC, git edge, bucket sync); 12 weeks = tokens + thin sync service + two sandbox exec adapters — anything claiming more than this from one developer is fiction, which is precisely why form factors (a) and (c) are traps. Primary grounding: /Users/apple/Projects/agentspaces/docs/agent-infra-research-archil-mesa-code.md, Sections 4–5.

form_factor_options.option: (a) FUSE/NFS mount daemon (Rust or Go) — kernel-adjacent POSIX mount over the workspace store
pros: Highest fidelity: every unmodified tool (compilers, git, grep, the harness itself) just works on mounted state
Only path to true fault-on-open lazy materialization — unlocks large-corpora use cases (report #21 ML datasets, #26 data rooms, #45 media)
NFSv3 loopback variant avoids macFUSE kext pain and works where FUSE is unavailable
Matches the premium UX Archil/Mesa charge for; strong moat if achieved
cons: Production bar is brutal: page-cache coherence, mmap, locking, fsync semantics, crash consistency — Archil bet 10 months of a whole company on just the protocol layer

FUSE is unavailable or privileged in most sandboxes (needs /dev/fuse + CAP_SYS_ADMIN; gVisor/Firecracker support varies) — undermines the mount-into-any-sandbox story for exactly the environments agents use
macOS story fragments across macFUSE/FSKit/NFS loopback — a support matrix one person cannot soak-test
Head-on competition with Archil where they are strongest; consumes the entire 12-week runway for one surface
form_factor_options.verdict: defer — month 4+ optional accessor (start with a 1-week read-only NFSv3 loopback spike serving a commit), never the v1 interface; pursuing this first is how a 1-person team dies

form_factor_options.option: (b) In-process SDK virtual filesystem (TypeScript first, Python later) — agents/harnesses call the store directly
pros: Zero kernel, zero privileges: works inside any sandbox, isolate, or just-bash simulated environment — the one surface that reaches P6 isolate-scale deployments
Naturally fully-lazy: reads pull manifests/chunks on demand, the cleanest lazy-materialization story of any form factor
The right embedding surface for platform builders (repo-per-user-session apps); Mesa's VFS+just-bash validates the pattern
Thin layer if generated over the same engine API as the CLI — low marginal cost once the engine exists
cons: Invisible to any process not linking the SDK: compilers, git, system grep, and the harness's own file tools cannot see VFS files unless materialized — standalone, it is a toy for coding agents
Ecosystem expects both TS and Python — double maintenance for a team of one
As a standalone wedge it competes with Mesa's exact play with 1/10th the capital and no GA pressure relief
form_factor_options.verdict: pursue as a component, not the wedge — TS SDK in weeks 5–8 as a thin binding over the engine's API, paired with materialize-to-workdir for everything outside the SDK; Python deferred

form_factor_options.option: (c) Git-protocol-compatible server — speak smart-HTTP/pack protocol so existing tools interop directly
pros: Instant interop with the entire human SDLC, CI, and IDE tooling — the moat-bearing edge per the report
Pierre proves platforms pay real money for exactly this
cons: Packfile negotiation and ref-transaction correctness at production grade is a multi-year systems effort — Pierre took ~3 years with ex-GitHub engineers; this is the single worst effort-to-wedge ratio available to us
Requires operating a server, contradicting the local-first wedge and adding on-call burden to a team of one
Adopting git's object model internally to make this easier poisons the engine: no chunking for large files, SHA-1/SHA-256 awkwardness, tree-granularity writes
form_factor_options.verdict: reject as a form factor — ship git interop at the edges instead: `ws git export/import` via gitoxide in v1, and a git remote-helper (`git push ws::...`) later, which delivers ~80% of the interop value at ~2% of the cost

form_factor_options.option: (d) Local CLI + content-addressed store with optional sync (SQLite-as-truth + BLAKE3/FastCDC CAS over object storage)
pros: The only engine architecture a 1-person+AI team can make genuinely production-grade in weeks: SQLite WAL gives transactions and multi-process safety on one host; crash-safety is testable with a kill -9 matrix
O(1) CoW branching falls out structurally: branch = one row pointing at an immutable commit; trees/chunks structurally shared; checkpoint-every-step has no ceremony cost
Single static musl binary doubles as the sandbox-injection mechanism — curl one file into any E2B/Modal/Daytona box and the workspace follows the agent

Zero-server sync: chunk replication is idempotent CAS upload; ref updates use S3 conditional writes (If-Match CAS) against a plain BYO bucket — durability scales from laptop to S3 with no service to operate
Open-sourceable like git itself — the only defensible posture against incumbent bundling

Beats the alternatives examined: git object model (no chunking, packfiles), jj-lib backend (API churn, human-VCS working-copy assumptions, Mesa owns the narrative), pure-CAS-no-DB (loses transactional metadata and fast status)
cons: A CLI alone has no distribution wedge — developers already have git; it needs (e) to reach users
Lazy materialization is approximate: path-scoped checkout + chunk fetch + dedup, not fault-on-open — large-corpora use cases out of reach until/unless (a)

The 'why not git worktrees?' objection arrives daily and must be answered crisply (O(1) fork of dirty state including untracked files, checkpoint without commit ceremony, non-code trees, agent-scale ref churn, no .git exposed to the agent)
form_factor_options.verdict: pursue — this is the engine; riskiest bet is snapshot/status latency on large dirty trees, so week-1 prototype is an mtime-indexed status scan + BLAKE3/FastCDC throughput benchmark on a 100k-file tree

form_factor_options.option: (e) MCP server exposing workspace operations (open/fork/checkpoint/diff/merge/exec) to agent harnesses
pros: Distribution through the agent ecosystem itself: `claude mcp add agentspaces` is a 30-second, no-account install — a channel none of the three competitors occupy (all are cloud-service-first)
The MCP tool surface maps 1:1 onto the report's Section-5 interface sketch — the product is literally the primitive
Pairs with workdir materialization: the harness's native Read/Edit/Bash tools operate on a real directory while fork/checkpoint/diff/merge/promote ride MCP — best of both interfaces
Compounds with the ecosystem: every harness (Claude Code, Codex, OpenCode) speaks MCP, and Anthropic's own guidance pushes state into the execution filesystem
Near-zero marginal cost as a `ws mcp` subcommand of the same binary — one artifact, no separate server
cons: Distribution-shaped risk, not tech risk: MCP servers are commodities; adoption depends on harness power users caring about branchable state
Per-tool-call overhead in long agent loops (mitigated: the harness can equally drive the CLI via Bash; MCP is discovery + ergonomics, CLI is the hot path)
Does not reach platform builders embedding workspaces in products — that is (b)'s job
Exposed to the harness vendor shipping native checkpoint/workspace semantics
form_factor_options.verdict: pursue — the wedge interface; validate by week 3 by dogfooding agentspaces to build agentspaces and recruiting 5–10 Claude Code power users

form_factor_options.option: (f) Combination: (d) engine + (e) MCP wedge + (b) SDK component + (c) reduced to git edges — one static binary
pros: One Rust binary = CLI + MCP stdio server + exec drivers + git edge, all reading the same SQLite+CAS store — one artifact to build, test, version, and inject into sandboxes
Covers all four audiences in priority order: harness users (MCP, week 2), scripts/CI (CLI, week 2), human SDLC (git edge, week 6), platform builders (TS SDK, week 8)
Each milestone is independently shippable: 2-week alpha, 6-week production local-first, 12-week sync+tokens+sandbox-adapters — no big-bang integration risk

Exec-attached-to-state without building a sandbox company: local subprocess → Docker → partner sandbox adapters, all the same fork→run→checkpoint contract with the run recorded in commit metadata as the audit log
cons: Three surfaces invite scope creep — requires ruthless discipline to keep (a), (c)-as-server, and hosted multi-tenant out of the first 12 weeks
Local-first means no revenue surface until the thin sync service exists (founder decision, not architecture)
form_factor_options.verdict: pursue — this is the recommendation

must_have: Single static Rust binary `ws` (musl): init, snapshot, fork, checkout (path-scoped), status, diff, log, merge, gc — fork and checkpoint must be O(1) and feel instant

Storage engine: SQLite (WAL) as truth for commit DAG, tree manifests, refs, mtime index, branch leases; BLAKE3 content-addressed chunk store with FastCDC chunking >1MB, whole-blob 4KB–1MB, inline ≤4KB in SQLite

Concurrency model: branch-per-agent by default (fork-on-open, Mesa's observe-mode insight); single-writer lease per branch with TTL in SQLite; readers always read immutable commits; 3-way text merge with conflict markers, never blocking
Built-in MCP stdio server (`ws mcp`): workspace_open / fork / checkpoint / diff / merge / log / exec / grep + materialize-to-workdir — tool descriptions written for harness ergonomics
Local exec driver: fork → materialize workdir → run subprocess → record (command, exit code, duration, env hash) in commit metadata → auto-checkpoint; sane defaults (workdir-jailed cwd)
Git at the edge: export branch to a real git repo / import a git repo, via gitoxide
BYO-bucket sync (week 6): idempotent chunk push/pull + ref compare-and-swap via S3/R2 conditional writes — zero servers operated
Capability-token format (workspace, branch-glob, path-glob, mode, ttl, exec) present in the API surface from day one, explicitly unenforced while local-first

Production evidence, not claims: kill -9 crash-recovery matrix in CI; benchmark suite proving sub-second status/fork on a 100k-file tree; quickstart that gets Claude Code from zero to forked-checkpointed-merged in under 5 minutes
defer: FUSE/NFS mount daemon — explicit go/no-go decision at month 4 after a 1-week read-only NFSv3 loopback spike
Git wire-protocol server — likely never; a git remote-helper delivers the interop instead
Hosted multi-tenant sync service + capability-token enforcement (thin Cloudflare Worker in front of R2) — weeks 10–12 at the earliest, gated on the founder's revenue-horizon answer
Sandbox exec adapters (E2B, Modal, Daytona): inject static binary, scoped-token checkout, run, push — weeks 9–12
TypeScript SDK alpha weeks 5–8 (cuttable); Python SDK post-v1
jj-style first-class conflict-carrying changes and op-log undo — v2 semantics upgrade, design the commit format so it does not preclude them
Web UI / diff viewer
Machine/memory snapshots — permanently out of scope: per the report, machine images are a derived cache of the workspace, not the layer of record

risks: Performance cliff is the riskiest technical bet of the whole plan: if fork + materialize + status is not sub-second on real repos (100k files, dirty trees, node_modules pathologies), the core promise — fork-per-attempt, checkpoint-every-step — dies regardless of how elegant the DAG is. Prototype the mtime-indexed status scan and BLAKE3/FastCDC throughput in week 1 before writing anything else.

Incumbent/vendor bundling (the report's own strongest counter-thesis): Anthropic shipping native workspace/checkpoint semantics in Claude Code, or E2B/Modal bundling branchable volumes, compresses the wedge. The only durable posture for a team of one is becoming the open format others adopt — which makes the founder's open-source decision load-bearing, not cosmetic.

Turso's AgentFS already occupies adjacent open-source mindshare ([github.com/tursodatabase/agentfs](https://github.com/tursodatabase/agentfs), [turso.tech/blog/agentfs](https://turso.tech/blog/agentfs), now with a FUSE option and WASM build) — it is isolation/audit-shaped (snapshot = copy the .db) rather than a branch/merge/diff DAG with git interop and fleet sync, but the positioning must be differentiated explicitly and early or we get read as a clone.

Honest-laziness gap: without the kernel layer, v1 cannot serve large-corpora use cases (ML datasets, data rooms, media pipelines — report #21/#26/#45). Marketing must scope claims to code- and document-sized trees or credibility burns on first contact.

The 'why not git worktrees?' objection will arrive in every single conversation; without a crisp documented answer (O(1) dirty-state fork including untracked files, checkpointing without commit ceremony, non-code trees, agent-scale ref churn, no .git foot-gun in the agent's view) the wedge stalls at hello.

Zero-server sync depends on conditional-write parity across S3/R2/GCS/MinIO; semantic drift between providers forces the thin enforcement service earlier than planned — verify with a 3-concurrent-writer torture test before committing the design.

Deferred token enforcement means no multi-tenant story until month 3+: correct for local-first, fatal if a platform-builder design partner shows up in week 4 — manage that expectation explicitly rather than improvising enforcement.

The local exec driver runs agent-chosen commands as a subprocess by default — without conservative defaults this becomes the security anecdote that defines the product; the box is not our trust boundary but we must not make the data layer a privilege-escalation path either.
MCP spec churn and per-call overhead in long loops; mitigated because the CLI is the hot path and MCP is discovery/ergonomics, but the binding code must be regenerable, not hand-grown.

One developer in Rust: crash-safety and durability claims must be earned in CI (kill-matrix, fault injection) rather than asserted, and the soak time for 'production-grade' is real — 6 weeks buys production-grade local-first, not production-grade distributed anything.

open_questions.question: Open-source posture for the engine: is agentspaces the 'git' (open core, sell the GitHub later) or a closed product from day one?
open_questions.why_it_matters: This is the load-bearing strategic decision. Against three funded cloud services and the bundling threat, an open local-first engine is the only position a 1-person team can defend — adoption becomes the moat and the sync/token/exec service becomes the business. But it forecloses some acquisition/pricing paths and commits to community overhead. Architecture is identical either way; everything about distribution, hiring leverage, and which risks bite is not.
options: Apache-2/MIT engine + future paid sync/cloud (the git/GitHub playbook — strongest fit with the MCP wedge)
Source-available (BSL/FSL) — hedged, but blunts the 'become the standard' defense against bundlers
Closed SaaS from day one — maximizes optionality on pricing, minimizes the wedge

open_questions.question: First customer for the first 12 weeks: harness power users (bottom-up, MCP-first) or agent-platform builders (B2B, SDK-first, repo-per-user-session)?
open_questions.why_it_matters: Decides the polish ordering between the MCP server and the TS SDK, whether token enforcement can genuinely wait, whether a hosted service is needed in 2026 at all, and what the demo is. The architecture serves both from one binary, but a team of one can only do developer-experience excellence for one audience at a time.
options: Claude Code/Codex power users first — fastest validation loop, dogfoodable while building, zero sales motion (the plan above assumes this)
Platform builders first — bigger contracts, but demands tokens + sync + SLAs immediately, i.e., Pierre's game with Pierre's costs
Sequential: harness users weeks 1–8, first design-partner platform weeks 9–12

open_questions.question: Revenue horizon and runway tolerance: can agentspaces stay pre-revenue, local-first + BYO-bucket through roughly Q4 2026?
open_questions.why_it_matters: A paid hosted sync service pulls capability-token enforcement, billing, multi-tenancy, and on-call into the 12-week window and roughly halves engine velocity. If runway or psychology demands revenue by week 12, the v1 scope above must shed the sandbox adapters and possibly the git edge to make room — that trade is the founder's to make, not the architect's.
options: Local-first through 2026, monetize in 2027 once the wedge has users
Thin paid sync service (Worker + R2) shipping at week 12
Design-partner contracts (one platform builder) funding hosted work early, accepting the velocity hit

open_questions.question: Appetite for the kernel-adjacent bet at month 4: commit 3–6 months to a FUSE/NFS mount, partner for mounts instead, or stay materialization-based indefinitely?
open_questions.why_it_matters: The mount is the only path to true fault-on-open laziness and the large-corpora third of the market, and it is the difference between 'workspace engine' and 'filesystem company.' It is also the single biggest schedule and reliability risk available to us, and it competes head-on with Archil's core strength. This is a taste-and-ambition call about what kind of company agentspaces is.
options: Commit at month 4 if the wedge has traction (start with the 1-week read-only NFSv3 loopback spike)
Partner: stay the version-semantics layer and let Archil-class mounts or sandbox vendors carry the bytes
Never: stay materialization-based and concede large corpora permanently

architect.recommendation: Ship agentspaces as one static Rust binary that is simultaneously the engine, the CLI, and an MCP server — form factor (f) = (d)+(e), with (b) as a later component and (c) reduced to git edges. Engine: SQLite-as-truth (commit DAG, tree manifests, refs, mtime index, branch leases) + BLAKE3 content-addressed chunk store with FastCDC chunking, giving O(1) copy-on-write fork/checkpoint structurally (branch = a row; everything beneath is immutable and shared) — explicitly rejecting the git object model and the jj-lib backend internally while stealing jj's semantics (fork-on-first-write, branch-per-agent, never-blocking merges) and keeping git interop at the boundary via gitoxide. Wedge: `ws mcp` exposes open/fork/checkpoint/diff/merge/exec/grep plus materialize-to-workdir directly to Claude Code-class harnesses — a 30-second, no-account, no-server install through the agent ecosystem itself, the one channel Archil, Mesa, and Pierre all leave open. Exec attaches to state without building a sandbox company: fork → materialize → run (local subprocess now; Docker, then E2B/Modal/Daytona adapters by injecting the same binary) → record the run in commit metadata → auto-checkpoint, so commits are the audit log by construction. Durability is local disk first with CI-enforced crash-safety, then zero-server BYO-bucket sync using S3/R2 conditional-write ref CAS at week 6; capability tokens are in the API from day one but unenforced until a thin Worker+R2 sync service makes enforcement real (weeks 10–12 or later, per the founder's revenue answer). Sequence: week 1 prototype the two riskiest bets (mtime-indexed sub-second status on 100k files; chunking throughput), week 2 alpha demoable in Claude Code, week 6 production-grade local-first, week 12 sync + tokens + two sandbox adapters. Defer the FUSE/NFS mount to an explicit month-4 decision and never build a git wire-protocol server. This is the git-not-GitHub play: the three funded competitors are all selling services above the primitive — we ship the primitive itself, where small, sharp, and local-first is an advantage rather than a handicap.

customer.lens: User / customer — who actually adopts this, what their first five minutes must feel like, what makes them churn, and what they will pay
customer.summary: Walking the four personas as real adopters, the ranking by (reachability for a tiny team) x (pain intensity) is: AI engineers running fork-N-compare-promote loops (#1), Claude Code/Codex power users (#2), agent-product developers needing per-user durable state (#3), enterprise platform teams (#4). The eval/fleet engineer's current workaround is cp -r, tarballs, and git worktrees — slow on multi-GB trees, disk-hungry, no cross-attempt diffing, no provenance — and their winning five minutes is 'brew install agentspaces; asp init in an existing directory; asp fork -n 8; run agents; asp diff shows a comparison table; asp promote 3' with zero signup, zero FUSE, zero daemon; they only adopt free OSS, but they are the single most reachable audience for a one-person team via an HN/X devtools launch. The Claude Code power user is served by the same artifact through 'claude mcp add agentspaces' — but Claude Code's native /rewind has already absorbed session-scoped undo, so the wedge for them is what the harness cannot do: durable cross-session timelines, parallel forks of whole workspaces (not just code edits), and diff/promote across concurrent agent sessions; they pay little ($0–20/mo) but are the loudest distribution channel, and critically the agent itself is the primary user — demanding verb-named tools (workspace_fork, workspace_diff, workspace_promote), --json on every command, and error strings that state the corrective next action ('fork \"attempt-3\" has uncommitted changes; run workspace_checkpoint first or pass force=true') so the model self-corrects instead of flailing. The agent-product developer duct-taping S3+Postgres (tarball-restore latency into sandboxes, silent concurrent-write clobbering, hand-rolled version tables) has the strongest willingness to pay — this is a real infra line item at roughly $0.15–0.30/GB-month equivalents — but is exactly who funded Mesa, Pierre, and Archil are courting with more capital; a tiny team wins them only after OSS-earned trust, with 'same CLI, but workspace.open(\"user-123\") is hosted, backed by your own S3 bucket' as the upgrade path, and open core is itself the trust strategy because no one bets their product's state layer on a one-person closed-source vendor. The enterprise platform team pays the most ($50k+/yr for audit trails and approval gates) and is structurally unreachable: procurement, SOC 2, SSO, and vendor-risk review all fail a team of one — harvest their requirements (append-only audit export, gate-on-promote) as roadmap and reach them later through persona 3's products. Adoption-friction realities cut hard and consistently: FUSE is disqualifying on macOS, any signup before the aha kills the top two personas, and Turso's AgentFS already occupies the 'SQLite agent filesystem' mindshare — which is why the differentiated, buildable wedge is not another storage substrate but the workspace control plane over files people already have: O(1) reflink/clonefile forks of real directories and git repos, checkpoint/undo/timeline, fork-N-compare-promote, git/jj-interoperable at the edge. Churn triggers are near-uniform across personas: any data loss (one strike, permanently fatal for a storage-adjacent tool), being slower than cp -r on small trees, fighting the user's existing git repo, or requiring an account or kernel module. The first five minutes must end in a felt aha — 'eight forks of a 5 GB workspace in under a second, and a diff table that names the winner' — or none of these users come back.

form_factor_options.option: A. Local-first OSS single-binary CLI + bundled MCP server: workspace verbs (init/fork/checkpoint/diff/promote/undo/timeline) layered over real directories and existing git repos via reflink/clonefile copy-on-write and a content-addressed sidecar store

skeptic.summary: Run the kill-list first: by June 2026 most of this category's obvious slices are already lost, and two of the report's "incumbent risk" scenarios have partially happened. AWS shipped Amazon S3 Files in April 2026 (buckets as ~1ms filesystems, explicitly pitched at agents), Tigris-style bucket forking brought GitHub-style forks to object storage, GitHub has Agent HQ, Turso's AgentFS is a free OSS copy-on-write agent filesystem with FUSE and browser builds, and Claude Code shipped native worktree isolation in April 2026 — so the hosted-POSIX-mount, hosted-versioned-FS, headless-git, and local-worktree-manager slices are respectively owned by AWS+Archil, Mesa+Turso, Pierre, and free OSS plus the harnesses themselves. Every one of the five failure vectors (capitalized competitors, incumbent compression, good-enough git, no sales motion, data-loss blast radius) points at the same conclusions: a solo dev must not operate a hosted multi-tenant storage fleet, must not compete on latency benchmarks, must not depend on platform sales, and must not be the source of truth for anyone's bytes in v1. What those constraints leave open is exactly one structural gap nobody is incented to fill: a user-owned, local-first, git-interoperable branchable-workspace engine — the Section 5 primitive implemented as a single binary over the user's own disk and bucket, where worst-case failure degrades to "you still have a plain git repo," not data loss. The hosted vendors won't build it (their business is custody), hyperscalers won't do version semantics, harness vendors won't do durable cross-substrate state, and git worktrees genuinely don't cover untracked files, large artifacts, audit trails, or fleet-scale fan-out. Distribution must be bottom-up OSS riding the harnesses (Claude Code hooks/MCP, OpenCode, Codex CLI), because that is the only channel a one-person team has. The honest residual risks are that Turso extends AgentFS to object storage and git interop (their blog signals this), that harness-native checkpointing keeps deepening, and that OSS adoption may never convert to revenue — which is why the founder's ambition and horizon questions below are the real decision, not the technical shape.
