skeptic.recommendation: Build the zero-custody version of the Section 5 primitive: an open-source, single-binary, local-first branchable workspace engine ("agentspaces") that gives any agent harness O(1) copy-on-write forks of real directories, commit-grained capture of every agent write (including untracked files and artifacts) into a shadow git repo, and a diff-then-promote approval gate that lands winners as ordinary git branches/PRs — distributed bottom-up via first-class Claude Code hooks/MCP integration and a plain CLI for every other harness. This is the only form factor that survives all five red-team vectors at once: it custodies no bytes (worst-case failure degrades to a plain git repo, the sole survivable blast radius for solo-built storage), it competes on semantics and trust rather than NVMe benchmarks, it needs no sales motion, it sits in the one structural gap none of the funded players or incumbents is incented to fill, and it is positioned to grow into the full primitive (BYO-bucket sync, capability tokens, paid team control plane) only after it has earned trust. Explicitly do NOT build: a hosted filesystem or versioned-FS service (Archil/Mesa/AWS S3 Files own it; custody kills you), headless git for platforms (Pierre owns it; wrong buyer), or a worktree-orchestration UX layer (already commoditized by free OSS and absorbed by the harnesses in 2026). The two clocks to respect: ship a credible v1 with published crash-safety torture tests inside ~10 weeks, because Turso's AgentFS trajectory and harness-native checkpointing are both converging on this ground.

scout.summary: Build-vs-reuse verdict: every layer of the branchable-workspace primitive except the product itself already exists as a permissively-licensed, actively-maintained library, and a 1-dev+AI team should assemble, not invent. The version engine is free (git's object model via gitoxide or plain git plumbing gives content-addressed trees, O(1) branches, diff/merge, and git interop at the edge by construction — the thing Pierre charges for and Mesa had to build a translation layer for). The large-file/cold tier is free (FastCDC + BLAKE3 + SQLite manifest + R2, the restic/bup recipe, all MIT/BSD). The genuinely hard, fleet-scale parts — Archil's NVMe cache protocol, Pierre's quorum ref store, Mesa's jj-at-scale — are exactly the parts a wedge product can defer by being LOCAL-FIRST: state lives in a per-workspace CAS on the developer's machine or a single-tenant volume, syncs to object storage, and reaches agents through three progressively heavier surfaces: (1) CLI + MCP server (distribution via `claude mcp add` / .mcp.json / the now-API-frozen MCP registry — a one-person-shippable channel no competitor in the report owns), (2) in-process VFS for just-bash/Node sandboxes (Apache-2.0, validated by Mesa pairing with it), (3) a localhost userspace NFSv3 mount (nfsserve, BSD-3, proven by XetHub and copied by both ZeroFS and Turso's AgentFS for macOS) — the no-kext mount path that sidesteps FSKit, which is currently broken for third parties on macOS 26 by an Apple bug. Key landscape facts verified today: Turso AgentFS (MIT, beta v0.6.4, 3.2k stars) is the nearest collision — local SQLite-file workspaces with CoW branching and FUSE/NFS mounts — but it is local-only and not git-interoperable; its S3-disaggregated version is explicitly 'a direction, not a finished system' (Penberg, Jan 2026). That gap — durable, git-interoperable, cloud-synced, sandbox-mountable — is the open seam. Mount-into-any-sandbox is NOT uniform (Modal's gVisor forbids FUSE; E2B needs a custom template; Daytona has first-class FUSE volumes; Fly is full VMs), so the architecture must treat the real mount as the premium tier and degrade gracefully to VFS/sync — which conveniently matches the build order a solo dev needs anyway. Recommended stack: Rust core daemon (gitoxide engine — start with plain git plumbing + worktrees + APFS clonefile for week-1 credibility), FastCDC/BLAKE3/SQLite/R2 CAS for large files, TypeScript MCP server as the distribution face, nfsserve mount as v2. Roughly 8–12 weeks to a production v1 without the mount; the mount layer is the single riskiest component in every stack and should never block the first release.

building_blocks.name: Plain git plumbing (subprocess: worktrees, partial clone, bundles, cat-file --batch)
building_blocks.what_it_is: The git CLI driven as a subprocess: `git worktree` for O(1)-ish parallel checkouts, `--filter=blob:none` partial clones for lazy materialization, bundles for offline transport, hash-object/mktree/commit-tree for synthetic commits without a working tree.
building_blocks.how_it_helps: Gives the entire version core of the primitive (branch = fork, commit = audit log, diff = review gate) with zero code, total battle-testing, and perfect ecosystem interop. Worktrees are already the harness-native parallelism idiom (Claude Code ships EnterWorktree). Synthetic-commit plumbing lets the daemon commit every agent write without a checkout.
building_blocks.maturity: The most battle-tested software in this domain; stable for decades; partial clone stable since ~2.27.
building_blocks.license: GPLv2, but subprocess invocation imposes no license coupling on your code.
building_blocks.verdict: use — this is the correct week-1 engine; replace hot paths with gitoxide later, not before profiling proves the fork cost matters.

building_blocks.name: gitoxide (gix)
building_blocks.what_it_is: Pure-Rust idiomatic git implementation; gix 0.84.0 (May 26, 2026), actively developed, powers cargo's git operations and is used by GitButler/Radicle. Read paths (odb, refs, trees, diff) are strong; merge landed recently; push/server-side paths still maturing per crate-status.md.
building_blocks.how_it_helps: In-process git object model: content-addressed trees, refs-as-branches, diffs — no subprocess overhead, no libgit2 C FFI, embeddable in a daemon that does thousands of micro-commits (one per agent write).
building_blocks.maturity: Pre-1.0 but production-adjacent; mixed per-crate (gix-lock/tempfile production-grade, write/transport paths younger); very active (monthly releases through 2026).
building_blocks.verdict: use — as the in-process engine for the Rust core in phase 2; keep behind your own Workspace trait so the git-plumbing fallback stays available.

building_blocks.name: Jujutsu jj-lib
building_blocks.what_it_is: The library crate behind jj (Apache-2.0, 29.5k stars, very active — pushed today). 0.42.0 (June 4, 2026). Explicitly designed for non-CLI consumers; change-based DAG, fork-on-write, first-class non-blocking conflicts; has a git backend that stores commits in a real git repo.
building_blocks.how_it_helps: Its semantics ARE the report's Section-5 primitive (Mesa's whole engine bet): every write a change, observe-then-fork, conflicts that don't block agents. The git backend means jj semantics with git interop for free.
building_blocks.maturity: Pre-1.0 with explicit warning of backward-incompatible on-disk format changes before 1.0; API surface acknowledged as unpolished.
building_blocks.verdict: consider — steal the semantic model (fork-on-first-write, change IDs over commit IDs) and implement it as a thin layer over git refs yourself; taking the crate dependency means riding pre-1.0 format breaks AND fighting Mesa on Mesa's chosen ground.

building_blocks.name: libgit2
building_blocks.what_it_is: The canonical embeddable C git library with bindings in every language (git2-rs, pygit2, nodegit).
building_blocks.how_it_helps: Mature in-process git if the core were C/Python/Node; powers GitHub/GitLab internals historically.
building_blocks.maturity: Very mature, slower-moving; known performance ceilings on huge repos; git2-rs maintained.
building_blocks.license: GPLv2 WITH linking exception (effectively permissive for dynamic use, but legal review friction).
building_blocks.verdict: avoid — gitoxide dominates it for a new Rust core (no FFI, better perf trajectory, cleaner license); only relevant if you abandoned Rust.

building_blocks.name: isomorphic-git + LightningFS / memfs
building_blocks.what_it_is: Pure-JS git (MIT, mature, maintained) plus in-memory/IDB-backed fs shims for Node and browsers.
building_blocks.how_it_helps: Lets a TypeScript SDK do real git operations inside an isolate/edge runtime with no native deps — the P6 (isolates) surface of the primitive.
building_blocks.maturity: Stable but slow on large repos (no packfile mmap, JS hashing); memfs is the standard Node fs shim; LightningFS browser-focused.
building_blocks.verdict: consider — only for an edge/browser surface later; do not make it the engine, large-repo performance is disqualifying.

building_blocks.name: FastCDC (fastcdc-rs) / restic chunker
building_blocks.what_it_is: Content-defined chunking: fastcdc crate 4.0.1 (Apr 2026, MIT, Nathan Fiedler) implementing FastCDC 2016/2020; restic's Go chunker (BSD-2) is the same idea, a decade in production.
building_blocks.how_it_helps: Dedup for files git handles badly (datasets, media, node_modules, model weights): chunk → BLAKE3 → CAS. This is the entire 'pay only for unique bytes per branch' economics of Archil's branch billing, as a library.
building_blocks.maturity: Algorithm and crates both mature; restic proves the design at exabyte aggregate scale.
building_blocks.verdict: use — fastcdc-rs for the large-file tier alongside (not inside) git objects.

building_blocks.name: BLAKE3
building_blocks.what_it_is: Official blake3 crate from the BLAKE3 team; SIMD/multithreaded, ~10x SHA-256 throughput; verified streaming via its Bao tree mode.
building_blocks.how_it_helps: Content addresses for the CAS tier; tree mode enables verified partial/lazy reads of large blobs — the lazy-materialization property at the hash level.
building_blocks.maturity: Production-grade, used by Iroh, bao, cargo ecosystem; stable since 2020.
building_blocks.verdict: use — uncontroversial.

building_blocks.name: SQLite / libsql
building_blocks.what_it_is: Embedded SQL (public domain) and Turso's MIT fork adding embedded replicas and a sync protocol.
building_blocks.how_it_helps: The manifest/metadata store: path→chunk maps, branch heads, lease/lock tables, audit log, token scopes. One file per workspace = trivially snapshottable (AgentFS's whole trick). libsql sync offers a shortcut to multi-device metadata replication.
building_blocks.maturity: SQLite is the most deployed software on earth; libsql production at Turso (note: Turso is a direct competitor via AgentFS — using their sync service creates an awkward dependency; the embedded library is fine).
building_blocks.verdict: use — vanilla SQLite (rusqlite) for v1; treat libsql sync as optional and replaceable.

building_blocks.name: object_store crate + S3/R2
building_blocks.what_it_is: Apache Arrow's Rust abstraction over S3/GCS/Azure/R2/local with conditional puts; R2 has zero egress fees.
building_blocks.how_it_helps: The cold tier. Conditional-put (If-None-Match) on a manifest pointer gives cheap optimistic concurrency for branch heads without running a coordination service. R2's zero egress is what makes 'mount from any sandbox vendor' economically survivable for a startup.
building_blocks.maturity: Production-grade, used across the Rust data ecosystem (DataFusion, Delta-rs, SlateDB).
building_blocks.verdict: use — R2 default for egress economics, S3-compatible everywhere.

building_blocks.name: OSTree / casync / bup
building_blocks.what_it_is: Linux image/backup CAS systems: OSTree (LGPLv2+, powers Fedora Silverblue/Flatpak), casync (LGPL, effectively dormant since ~2019), bup (LGPL, git-packfile backup).
building_blocks.how_it_helps: Prior art only: OSTree's hardlink-farm checkouts are the fast-materialization trick worth copying; bup proves git packfiles can hold chunked big files.
building_blocks.maturity: OSTree mature but Linux-only and image-oriented; casync abandoned-ish; bup niche.
building_blocks.license: LGPL family — viral friction for static linking.
building_blocks.verdict: avoid as dependencies; mine OSTree's checkout strategy and bup's packfile layout for design.

building_blocks.name: fuser (Rust FUSE)
building_blocks.what_it_is: Maintained Rust FUSE bindings, 0.17.0 (Feb 2026).
building_blocks.how_it_helps: The Linux mount surface: real POSIX file tree backed by your CAS+git engine inside E2B custom templates, Daytona, Fly machines, and Linux desktops.
building_blocks.maturity: Solid and maintained; FUSE itself is decades old. Caveats live in the consumers: Modal (gVisor) cannot FUSE at all; E2B requires a custom template with FUSE installed; macOS needs the macFUSE kext (install friction).
building_blocks.verdict: use — for the Linux/sandbox mount tier in v2; never as the only access path.

building_blocks.name: nfsserve (userspace NFSv3 server)
building_blocks.what_it_is: xetdata's Rust NFSv3 server library, 0.11.0 (Apr 1, 2026), BSD-3-Clause: implement one NFSFileSystem trait, get a localhost-mountable server. Built by XetHub to mount multi-TB repos; forked by ZeroFS (zerofs_nfsserve) and the same NFS-not-FUSE trick is how Turso AgentFS mounts on macOS.
building_blocks.how_it_helps: THE no-kext mount path: macOS and Linux both mount localhost NFS natively, no kernel extension, no macFUSE, no FSKit. One trait implementation serves laptop and sandbox alike.
building_blocks.maturity: Niche but proven in anger by three independent products; NFSv3 protocol is fossilized-stable. Known limits: no file locking in v3, weaker cache-coherence knobs than FUSE, localhost-TCP perf ceiling (fine for agent workloads).
building_blocks.verdict: use — primary mount surface for macOS and the universal fallback; pair with fuser on Linux where FUSE is allowed.

building_blocks.name: macOS FSKit
building_blocks.what_it_is: Apple's user-space filesystem framework (macOS 15.4+), the sanctioned no-kext path Archil uses for its macOS app.
building_blocks.how_it_helps: Would be the first-class macOS mount — eventually.
building_blocks.maturity: Currently broken for ALL third-party extensions on macOS 26 (confirmed Apple bug affecting even Apple's own FSKitSample, per developer forums and public issue trackers, June 2026), plus DiskArbitration probing limitations that force mount -F workflows.
building_blocks.verdict: avoid for v1 — re-evaluate when Apple ships the fix; nfsserve covers macOS meanwhile, which is exactly the call Turso made.

building_blocks.name: go-fuse / go-nfs
building_blocks.what_it_is: Go equivalents of the mount stack (hanwen/go-fuse BSD; willscott/go-nfs Apache-2.0).
building_blocks.how_it_helps: Same surfaces if the core were Go.
building_blocks.maturity: Both mature and maintained.
building_blocks.verdict: avoid — only relevant in a Go stack, and Rust wins this domain on gitoxide+fastcdc+blake3+nfsserve all being native.

building_blocks.name: APFS clonefile + XFS/btrfs reflink + overlayfs
building_blocks.what_it_is: OS-level copy-on-write: `cp -c` on APFS, FICLONE on XFS/btrfs, overlayfs writable layers. Cognition's blockdiff snapshots 20 GB in ~200 ms via XFS reflink.
building_blocks.how_it_helps: Instant local branch materialization: a workspace fork becomes a clonefile of the checkout plus a ref — sub-second worktree forks on dev laptops, the blockdiff trick for sandbox-side env caches.
building_blocks.maturity: Kernel/OS facilities; rock solid where present (APFS everywhere on macOS; ext4 lacks reflink — must degrade to hardlinks).
building_blocks.verdict: use — clonefile/reflink in the local materializer from day one; overlayfs sandbox-side only.

building_blocks.name: Vercel just-bash + bash-tool
building_blocks.what_it_is: TypeScript-simulated bash with an in-memory/pluggable virtual filesystem (Apache-2.0 per npm, v3.0.1, 3.7k stars, active — pushed June 4, 2026); Vercel's bash-tool ships agent context-retrieval on top (Jan 2026).
building_blocks.how_it_helps: The zero-infrastructure execution surface: plug the workspace SDK in as the fs backing and an agent gets `grep/sed/cat` over a lazily-materialized branch with no container, no mount, no sandbox vendor. Mesa already validates exactly this pairing — it is table stakes, not differentiation.
building_blocks.maturity: Young but Vercel-backed and moving fast; command coverage broad but simulated (no real binaries).
building_blocks.license: Apache-2.0 (npm metadata; repo LICENSE file not detected by GitHub API — pin the npm release and keep a copy of the license assertion).
building_blocks.verdict: use — as the day-one 'try it in 30 seconds' surface for Node harnesses.

building_blocks.name: MCP SDKs (TypeScript/Python) + official registry
building_blocks.what_it_is: MIT-licensed official SDKs; the MCP Registry (registry.modelcontextprotocol.io) hit API freeze v0.1 with reverse-DNS namespacing and standardized install metadata; harnesses install servers via `claude mcp add`, project-level .mcp.json, and registry discovery.
building_blocks.how_it_helps: Distribution — the scarcest resource for a 1-dev company. A workspace MCP server (fork/checkpoint/diff/exec/token tools + file resources) rides into Claude Code, Cursor, and Codex-class harnesses through an existing channel none of Archil/Mesa/Pierre owns as their wedge. Anthropic's own code-execution-with-MCP guidance pushes state into the filesystem, which is precisely the product.
building_blocks.maturity: SDKs production-grade; registry in preview but API-frozen and multi-vendor-backed (Anthropic, GitHub, Microsoft).
building_blocks.verdict: use — this is the go-to-market layer; the MCP server IS the v1 product.

building_blocks.name: Exec delegation: E2B / Daytona / Modal / Fly Sprites / local Docker
building_blocks.what_it_is: Sandbox SDKs as compute consumers of the workspace. Verified surface differences: E2B supports FUSE via custom templates; Daytona has first-class FUSE-based volume mounts of object storage; Modal runs gVisor and cannot FUSE (SDK/sync path only); Fly Sprites/Machines are full VMs (anything mounts, 1–2 s create, sleep-without-metering).
building_blocks.how_it_helps: Delivers 'compute is a verb' without operating compute: ws.exec() = pick substrate, materialize branch (mount where possible, push-pull sync where not, local subprocess for the laptop case), run, commit results. Local Docker + subprocess covers the free tier.
building_blocks.maturity: All GA and venture-scaled (E2B hundreds of millions of sessions; Modal 1B+ sandboxes).
building_blocks.verdict: use — integrate two in v1 (local subprocess + Fly or E2B); the non-uniform mount story is exactly why the workspace, not the sandbox, must own state.

building_blocks.name: Turso AgentFS
building_blocks.what_it_is: MIT, beta v0.6.4 (Mar 2026), 3.2k stars: each agent's files+KV+toolcall audit in one SQLite file; CoW branches; FUSE mount on Linux, NFS on macOS; TS/Python/Rust SDKs.
building_blocks.how_it_helps: Primarily a collision and a validation: it proves demand for the local branchable workspace and proves the NFS-on-macOS trick. Its gaps define the differentiation: no object-storage durability (disaggregated design is a published research direction, not shipped), no git interop (SQLite file, not a repo — invisible to GitHub/CI/diff tooling), no exec attachment, single-box.
building_blocks.maturity: Beta, explicitly so; fast-moving with Turso's distribution behind it.
building_blocks.verdict: avoid as a dependency (coupling your core to a competitor's beta engine), and avoid replicating its local-SQLite shape — build the durable, git-interoperable, sandbox-mountable layer it conspicuously lacks, and ship before their disaggregation lands.

building_blocks.name: Dagger / BuildKit caching model
building_blocks.what_it_is: Apache-2.0 content-addressed DAG cache for build operations (LLB graph, per-op caching, lazy refs).
building_blocks.how_it_helps: Design lesson only: their cache treats environments as deterministic functions of inputs — the report's 'machine image as memoization of lockfiles.' Copy the idea for env-derivation (lockfile hash → cached snapshot); do not adopt the machinery, it is build-graph-shaped, not workspace-shaped.
building_blocks.maturity: Production-grade (BuildKit underpins Docker).
building_blocks.verdict: avoid as dependency, learn from for the derived-env cache feature.

building_blocks.name: Devcontainers spec + git worktrees (as conventions)
building_blocks.what_it_is: devcontainer.json as the de-facto environment definition; worktrees as the existing human convention for parallel branches.
building_blocks.how_it_helps: Free interop: read devcontainer.json to derive exec environments; present forks as worktree-like directories so humans and existing tools (IDEs, harness worktree features) understand the layout natively.
building_blocks.maturity: Both ubiquitous conventions.
building_blocks.verdict: use — as compatibility surfaces, not components.

building_blocks.name: ZeroFS
building_blocks.what_it_is: AGPL-3.0 Rust project (1.9k stars, active — pushed today): NFS/9P/NBD served directly over S3 via SlateDB, encryption + compression, built largely by one person.
building_blocks.how_it_helps: Existence proof that the exact architecture of the recommended stack — userspace NFS over object storage in Rust — is one-developer-feasible; its BSD-3 nfsserve fork carries useful protocol fixes upstreamable to your copy.
building_blocks.maturity: Young but real (packaged in FreeBSD ports, GitHub Action for CI volumes).
building_blocks.license: AGPL-3.0 — disqualifying as a dependency for a commercial product.
building_blocks.verdict: avoid as code, study as the closest open-source analogue of the storage layer (minus versioning — which is exactly your differentiation).

stack_options.stack: A. 'agentspaced' — local-first Rust workspace daemon, git-native core, MCP-first distribution (RECOMMENDED)
components: Rust daemon + CLI (single static binary, brew/curl install)

Engine v0: plain git plumbing via subprocess (synthetic commits per write-batch, refs as forks, worktree+APFS clonefile/reflink materialization); engine v1: gitoxide (gix) in-process behind the same Workspace trait
Large-file tier: fastcdc + blake3 + rusqlite manifest, git tracks pointers (LFS-compatible at the edge)
Cold/durable tier: object_store → Cloudflare R2 (conditional-put on branch heads for concurrency), git push interop to GitHub as the trust anchor
Surface 1 (v1 product): TypeScript MCP server exposing fork/checkpoint/diff/promote/exec/token + file tools; .mcp.json + MCP registry distribution
Surface 2: just-bash fs-backend adapter for in-process Node sandboxes
Surface 3 (v2): nfsserve localhost NFSv3 mount (macOS + universal fallback) and fuser FUSE (Linux/E2B/Daytona)
Exec delegation: local subprocess/Docker first, then Fly Machines or E2B mounting the workspace; Modal via sync-in/commit-out
stack_options.effort: 8–12 weeks to production v1 (CLI + MCP server + CAS + R2 sync + git interop + local exec — no network mount); +4–6 weeks for the NFS/FUSE mount tier as v2. Week 2 should already demo 'agent forks workspace, runs tests in 3 parallel branches, you review diffs and promote one' inside Claude Code.
stack_options.risk: The mount layer is the single riskiest component — POSIX edge cases, cache coherence between mount and CLI views, and NFSv3's missing locking; mitigated by making it v2 and never the only path. Second-order risk: per-write commit granularity bloating the git odb (mitigate by write-batching + periodic repack).
stack_options.verdict: Commit to this. It is the only stack where every component is permissive, proven, and 1-dev-sized; git-native means Pierre-grade interop for free and a story incumbents' SQLite-file and proprietary-protocol approaches cannot match; local-first means no fleet to operate before revenue; MCP means distribution without a partnerships team. It occupies precisely the seam Turso has published intentions toward but not shipped.

stack_options.stack: B. TypeScript-only 'workspace SDK + cloud git backbone' (Mesa-shape, lighter)
components: TS SDK with pluggable VFS (lazy materialization over HTTP range reads)
just-bash as the execution surface; memfs for tests
Backbone: plain bare git repos on a single Fly volume (or Hetzner) fronted by a thin Node/Bun API; isomorphic-git only for edge cases
R2 for large objects (LFS-style pointers)
MCP TS server sharing the SDK
E2B/Vercel Sandbox for delegated exec via sync-in/push-out
stack_options.effort: 5–8 weeks to production v1 — fastest credible path; one language, no Rust, no mount layer ever.
stack_options.risk: No real-mount path is the riskiest 'component' (an absence): the moment a customer's agent needs `npm install` or a real toolchain against the workspace, you are sync-shipping file trees into sandboxes and have rebuilt rsync; also lands you on Mesa's exact roadmap with less engine underneath.
stack_options.verdict: Take only if shipping in June matters more than defensibility — or fold it into Stack A as the MCP/SDK front-end (the recommended plan effectively contains this stack as its first deliverable).

stack_options.stack: C. jj-lib semantic engine over object storage (the 'out-Mesa Mesa' play)
components: Rust core embedding jj-lib 0.42 with its git backend (jj semantics, git storage)
Custom jj backend targeting object_store/R2 for lazy tree reads
nfsserve/fuser mount tier as in Stack A
MCP + CLI surfaces as in Stack A
stack_options.effort: 12–18 weeks to production v1, plus permanent tax tracking pre-1.0 jj-lib API and on-disk format breaks across its monthly releases.
stack_options.risk: jj-lib instability is the riskiest component — the project explicitly promises backward-incompatible format changes before 1.0, and a custom storage backend sits on its least-stable internal trait surface; strategically you also fight Mesa on the ground they chose with $5M and a head start.
stack_options.verdict: Avoid as the v1 engine; correct as a v3 evolution — Stack A's Workspace trait should be designed so jj-grade change semantics (stable change IDs, fork-on-first-write, non-blocking conflicts) can be layered over git refs without adopting jj's storage.

stack_options.stack: D. Assemble-on-vendors: wrap Archil or code.storage (or AgentFS) and build only the workspace UX
components: Archil disks (branches + disk.exec) or code.storage repos (ephemeral branches, commit-from-diff) as the engine
Thin MCP server + CLI + policy/approval layer on top
Harness integrations and token scoping as the entire product
stack_options.effort: 2–4 weeks to a working v1 — almost all glue.
stack_options.risk: Platform dependency is existential, not a component risk: the report itself predicts each vendor is 'one feature launch away' from shipping your wrapper as a feature (Archil already sells exec; Pierre already sells tokens); margins are theirs, roadmap is theirs.
stack_options.verdict: Avoid as the company; use as the competitive benchmark and possibly as an optional 'bring your own backend' enterprise adapter behind Stack A's interface once the native engine is the default.

competitor_updates: Turso AgentFS is further along than the report's passing mention: MIT-licensed, 3.2k stars, beta v0.6.4 (Mar 2026), 58 releases — single-SQLite-file agent workspaces with filesystem+KV+toolcall-audit, CoW branches, FUSE mount on Linux and NFS mount on macOS, TS/Python/Rust SDKs. It is the closest open-source collision with a local-first wedge — but it is local/single-box, not git-interoperable, and has no durable cloud tier.

Turso's S3 ambition is public but unshipped: Penberg's 'Towards a Disaggregated Agent Filesystem on Object Storage' (Jan 11, 2026) sketches SQLite-WAL-to-S3 sync with a coordinator and explicitly calls it 'a direction, not a finished system,' with open questions on write concurrency and large files. The durable/cloud-synced/git-interoperable seam is still open — for now.

macOS FSKit is currently broken for ALL third-party filesystem extensions on macOS 26 (confirmed Apple bug, reproduced even with Apple's own FSKitSample; plus standing DiskArbitration probing limitations). Practical consequence: the no-kext macOS mount path in 2026 is a localhost userspace NFSv3 server — the approach XetHub pioneered (nfsserve, BSD-3, v0.11.0 Apr 2026, actively maintained) and that both ZeroFS and Turso AgentFS independently adopted. macFUSE is also growing an FSKit backend as a hedge.

'Mount into any sandbox' is not uniform across exec vendors: Modal's gVisor sandboxes cannot use FUSE at all (SDK/sync paths only); E2B supports FUSE but only via custom templates; Daytona now ships first-class FUSE-based volume mounts of object storage; Fly Sprites/Machines are full VMs where anything mounts. Any workspace product must degrade gracefully from mount → in-process VFS → sync, which favors architectures where the mount is one surface among several rather than the core interface.

Vercel's just-bash has become real infrastructure: Apache-2.0 (npm v3.0.1), 3.7k stars, active through June 2026, with Vercel's bash-tool for filesystem-based context retrieval launched on top (Jan 2026, InfoQ coverage). Mesa pairing its VFS with just-bash is now table stakes — an in-process VFS adapter is mandatory, not differentiating. Caveat: the GitHub repo lacks a detected LICENSE file; the npm metadata asserts Apache-2.0 — pin versions.

The official MCP Registry reached API freeze (v0.1) with reverse-DNS namespacing, standardized install metadata, and backing from Anthropic, GitHub, and Microsoft — making MCP a stable, ownable distribution channel for a workspace product into Claude Code/Cursor-class harnesses; none of Archil/Mesa/Pierre currently leads with this channel.

Freestyle has sharpened into a direct messaging competitor: now positioned as 'VMs and Git for AI agents' with an explicit 'The best filesystem for AI agents is Git' engineering blog and a multi-tenant API-first git platform (scoped permissions, agent workspaces, GitHub sync) — crowding the git-as-agent-filesystem narrative from the platform side, though still sandbox-bundled rather than local-first/harness-native.

ZeroFS (AGPL-3.0, 1.9k stars, active daily) is a one-person-built NFS/9P/NBD-over-S3 filesystem on SlateDB with encryption and compression — an existence proof that the userspace-NFS-over-object-storage architecture is solo-feasible, and a license-incompatible neighbor whose nfsserve fork (zerofs_nfsserve) carries protocol fixes worth tracking.

Library freshness verified June 10, 2026: jj-lib 0.42.0 (Jun 4) still pre-1.0 with promised on-disk format breaks; gitoxide gix 0.84.0 (May 26) actively maturing with merge landed and write/server paths younger; fuser 0.17.0 (MIT), fastcdc 4.0.1 (MIT), nfsserve 0.11.0 (BSD-3) all maintained in 2026 — the entire recommended Rust bill of materials is permissive and alive.
